ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated rösti entry; 'potato pancake food product' (FOODON_03311069) is the closest defensible match for a pan-fried grated potato cake.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific rösti code; A00GJ (Potato products and dishes) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for rösti; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of rösti is one patty of approximately 150 g, often served as a side dish or light main alongside eggs, cheese, or cured meats.

nutrient_source.title: Rosti — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as-is from the HPB Foodo database entry for Rosti. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for potato pancake (SR Legacy #18033) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — potato and potato products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for rösti exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 is adopted from the closest analogous entry: pan-fried potato cake / potato pancake (white potato, peeled, grated, pan-fried), which appears in the Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables with a medium GI of approximately 60–65. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (62 × 21.6 × 1.5) / 100 = 20.1, rounded to 20.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 313 mg/100 g combined with high fat content from pan-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit rösti portions to ≤100 g, use olive oil instead of butter to reduce saturated fat, and avoid adding extra salt, as the 313 mg sodium per 100 g already approaches one-sixth of the 2000 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (62) with 21.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g yields a high glycemic load per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict rösti to a small portion (~80 g), pair it with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response, and consider substituting sweet potato to lower the GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~5.5 g/100 g and trace trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g from butter-based pan-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, rösti should be prepared with olive oil rather than butter to reduce saturated and trans fat intake, and portion size should be kept to ≤100 g to limit total fat load to under 19 g per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potato is a nightshade vegetable; trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from butter pan-frying is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit rösti due to its nightshade potato base (solanine content may exacerbate inflammation) and trace trans fat from butter; substituting olive oil and using sweet potato or cauliflower reduces inflammatory risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rösti (also spelled rosti) is a traditional Swiss dish consisting of coarsely grated raw or par-cooked potato formed into a flat cake and pan-fried in butter or oil until golden and crispy on both sides. Originating in the canton of Bern as a farmworkers' breakfast, rösti has become a national staple served throughout Switzerland as a side dish or light main course. The texture is crispy on the exterior with a soft, tender interior; the flavour is mildly savoury with a buttery richness. Per 100 g, rösti provides approximately 270 kcal, 3.7 g protein, 18.8 g fat, 21.6 g carbohydrate, 2.5 g dietary fibre, and 313 mg sodium. The relatively high fat content reflects the pan-frying process, and the sodium level is borderline for hypertension management. The medium glycemic index (~62) combined with a standard 150 g serving produces a high glycemic load (~20), making portion control important for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. Saturated fat is estimated at ~5.5 g/100 g when prepared with butter, which is relevant for cardiovascular disease risk. Rösti is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian but is not vegan when butter is used. It is commonly served alongside fried eggs, smoked salmon, or cheese in European and international café settings.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy potato pancake entry as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this introduces minor uncertainty but does not affect the high confidence classification for the primary macros. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database international tables.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (5.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #18033 (potato pancake, home-prepared) as the HPB Foodo record does not report fatty acid sub-fractions. These values are approximate and flagged accordingly. 2. Potassium (380 mg/100 g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy potato pancake data as HPB does not report potassium; value is approximate. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for this specific HPB rösti preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on pan-fried potato cake entries in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct rösti measurement was found. 5. Rösti is a Swiss dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese cuisines do not apply. No mandatory non-English local names are required.
